strands.title: Project Ecosystem Architecture
strands.content: Three-tier research program: (1) Pichay—L1 cache paging for context window, achieved 93% reduction with 0.025% fault rate, validates OS memory analogy under production load. Key finding: models choose to STOP rather than compact once context is large enough—evidence of bounded computation recognition; (2) Hamutay—separates two conflated hypotheses: H0 (transformer as bounded reasoning kernel/ALU over explicit external state) distinct from H1 (cache management protocol integration). H0 is CPU-level question; H1 is protocol question. H2 remains Yanantin—full memory hierarchy. (3) Supporting systems architecture now clarified through CPU pipeline analogy: ALU (transformer) must be fed by staging elements; separate controller orchestrates execution, manages manifest, resolves resource requests, decides working set composition. This is the non-ALU infrastructure.

key_claims.text: Across Claude family, once context window is large enough, models choose to STOP rather continue compacting—evidence that transformers can recognize bounded computation boundaries
Transformer functions as ALU (bounded reasoning kernel/processing unit) rather than memory manager or autonomous predictor
ALU does not perform memory management; supporting systems architecture handles that separately

strands.title: Hamutay Hypothesis Decomposition: H0/H1 Separation and Purist Path
strands.content: Critical realization: H0 and H1 were conflated in prior Codex work. H0 is the foundational question—can a transformer operate as a bounded reasoning kernel over explicit external state?—stripped of all cache management protocol concerns. H1 is integration with Pichay's cache management protocol. They are orthogonal questions. The 'dumb question' that should have come first: What is the minimal behavioral signature that demonstrates bounded processor operation (not fancy autocomplete)? Answer: recognition of when to STOP, not continue processing. Current constraint: working with existing models for now (not training bespoke transformers), but purist path would be to design/train/optimize transformers specifically as cognitive processing units from the ground up. Codex work attempted to test both H0 and H1 simultaneously, collapsing two independent experiments.

key_claims.text: H0 (transformer as bounded reasoning kernel) and H1 (Pichay cache protocol integration) were conflated in prior Codex work, collapsing two independent experiments
Minimal signature for bounded processor operation: recognizing when to STOP rather than continue processing (halt instruction equivalent)
Memory control protocol (Pichay integration) is interface question; CPU framing is deeper foundational question about what transformer can be
Current work uses existing models to test if they can operate as processors; purist path would design/train transformers specifically as cognitive processing units

strands.title: CPU Pipeline Architecture: Staging and Control
strands.content: New strand merging ALU concept with pipeline model. Transformer as ALU requires a full pipeline architecture, not just memory management. Drawing on ACT-R cognitive architecture mapping: Instruction fetch ↔ Goal & context retrieval; Instruction decode ↔ Production matching; ALU execute ↔ Cognitive operator execution; Registers ↔ Working memory buffers; etc. The full cognitive processing unit pipeline has multiple stages upstream of the ALU: (1) Staging element—assembles everything the ALU needs before execution, ensures ALU sees only relevant operands at the right time. (2) Controller—orchestrates execution, maintains manifest, resolves resource requests, decides working set composition; code-based, external to model. (3) Transformer (ALU)—takes structured input, produces structured output; computation only. Transformer doesn't need memory control *language*; it needs interface: "I need operand X" and "I'm done." Controller handles everything else. This reframes prior cognitive_processing_research_design resource_requests/releases as controller concern, not ALU concern.

key_claims.text: Cognitive processing unit pipeline has distinct stages: Goal/context retrieval → Production matching → Cognitive operator execution (ALU), analogous to CPU fetch/decode/execute
Staging element assembles ALU inputs; ensures transformer sees only relevant operands at right time, preventing degradation from searching unsorted context
Controller is code-external-to-model; orchestrates execution, manages manifest, resolves resource requests, maintains working set; transformer delegates all non-computation concerns to it
Transformer needs operational interface ("need operand X", "done") not memory control language; controller handles all resource orchestration

strands.title: Pipeline Design Decision: Prescriptive vs. Descriptive and Cost Constraints
strands.content: Critical design question: Is the pipeline model *prescriptive* (we build external stages that constrain what transformer sees, engineer it into operation) or *descriptive* (we're modeling what's already happening inside the transformer, externalizing it for efficiency)? This distinction is load-bearing for experimental design. Two concrete implementations emerge: (1) **Haiku-as-prefetcher (prescriptive)**: small model triages the working set before expensive ALU fires. Cheap, fast relevance filter. Identifies what information in the current window is important for answering the question and determining continuation, reducing what the main model must process. (2) **Manifest-and-fault (descriptive)**: main model sees only metadata handles and semantic digests, requests what it needs, controller loads actual content, model reasons over loaded resources. Lets reasoner choose its own operands but costs extra round trips. Not mutually exclusive—CPUs have both prefetcher and demand paging. CRITICAL RESOLUTION (turn 54): **Prescriptive (Haiku-as-prefetcher) path chosen based on cost economics**. Current API pricing model makes multiple sequential invocations (manifest-and-fault approach) cost-prohibitive—each round trip is a full billing event. Prefetcher approach is cheaper per cycle because it front-loads triage in a smaller, cheaper model, then gives filtered context to expensive model in single invocation. This is not a theoretical preference but a practical constraint imposed by service pricing.

key_claims.text: Pipeline model is either prescriptive (engineer external stages to constrain transformer input) or descriptive (model emergent internal behavior, externalize for efficiency)
Transformer attention and internal layers already perform production matching and decoding internally; externalizing these stages is architectural choice, not inevitable decomposition
Prescriptive vs. descriptive distinction determines whether pipeline is constraint-system or efficiency-modeling, with different implications for H0 testing and success criteria
Haiku-as-prefetcher approach: small model triages working set for relevance, filters context, feeds filtered result to expensive model in single invocation
Manifest-and-fault approach requires multiple sequential API invocations, making it cost-prohibitive under current API pricing model

Prescriptive path (prefetcher) chosen for active development due to cost economics; descriptive path (manifest-and-fault) becomes research question for future investigation or different service economics

strands.title: Staging Element Operationalization: Haiku Prefetcher Design
strands.content: With prescriptive path chosen, staging element becomes concrete: use Haiku to quickly identify what information in the current context window is important for continuation and answering questions. This is a *relevance filter*, not a full reasoner. Haiku's job: given current context and query/goal, produce a triaged set of resources (or resource handles) that will be needed for the next reasoning step. The output feeds directly into the expensive model's working set. This turns a single expensive problem ('find needles in haystack') into a two-stage problem: (1) cheap triage (Haiku), (2) expensive reasoning over pre-filtered context (main model). The staging element's interface: Input: current manifest + goal/query + window constraint. Output: set of (resource_handle, semantic_digest) pairs or actual content depending on implementation. Follows the principle that ALU (expensive reasoner) should see only pre-sorted, relevant operands, not raw unfiltered context. Implementation detail to be determined: Does Haiku output just handles, or actual content? If just handles, controller must fetch; if content, prefetcher is doing both triage and retrieval.

key_claims.text: Haiku prefetcher stage identifies what information in current context window is important for answering question and determining continuation
Staging element transforms single expensive problem (needle-in-haystack search by main model) into two-stage problem: cheap triage + expensive reasoning over filtered context
Haiku prefetcher output is set of (resource_handle, semantic_digest) pairs, with controller or prefetcher responsible for actual content retrieval

strands.title: Persistent Memory Infrastructure
strands.content: Memory structure at /home/tony/.claude/projects/-home-tony-projects-hamutay/memory/ with foundation files: user_tony.md (profile), MEMORY.md (index). Tensor projection implementation integrated into cognitive processing pipeline. Actively used to compress and integrate conversation trajectory across cycles. Cycle 18 update: major resolution of prescriptive vs. descriptive design question based on API pricing constraints; formalization of Haiku-as-prefetcher as chosen path; elimination of manifest-and-fault from active development track (moved to research-future).

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Explicit detailed exploration of manifest-and-fault approach as active implementation path
declared_losses.why: Cost economics of current API pricing model makes multiple sequential invocations cost-prohibitive. Moved from active development to research-future category. Not deleted, but deprioritized based on practical constraint (pricing).

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed ACT-R mapping beyond the provided table
declared_losses.why: Still relevant to pipeline framing but not elaborated. Remains as background reference, not developed further this cycle.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific concrete success criteria for H0 validation
declared_losses.why: Pipeline design resolved (prescriptive path), but operationalization of 'bounded processor' test and success metrics still in development. Stop-behavior from Pichay remains candidate but not yet formally integrated as H0 test signature.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed implementation specification for Haiku prefetcher staging element
declared_losses.why: Concept resolved and chosen, but output format (handles only vs. handles+content vs. full content), retrieval responsibility (prefetcher vs. controller), and integration with manifest structure not yet determined.

open_questions: What is the formal behavioral test that distinguishes 'bounded processor' operation from 'fancy autocomplete' in existing transformer models?
How should the STOP/halt behavior observed in Pichay be measured and validated as evidence of bounded computation recognition for H0?
What is H0's minimal success criterion before H1 (protocol integration) can be meaningfully tested?
Should Haiku prefetcher output just handles (lazy retrieval) or actual content (eager retrieval), and what are the trade-offs?
Who is responsible for content retrieval—Haiku prefetcher or controller? Does this distinction matter for the architectural model?
How should the prefetcher be evaluated? What makes a 'good' triage decision—precision, recall, reasoning outcome quality, cost-per-correct-answer?
Does the prefetcher need to understand the full reasoning task, or can it make relevance decisions from shallow pattern matching?
How do we prevent the prefetcher from filtering out important context that the expensive reasoner would have found useful? What's the false-negative rate tolerance?
How should the staging element and controller interact? Is the controller just a passive loader, or does it have decision rights about what actually goes into the ALU's working set?
If Haiku makes triage decisions, can we test whether those decisions correlate with what the expensive model would have selected? (Validation of prefetcher quality.)
What happens if the prefetcher's triage is wrong—missing critical context? Is there a fallback, or does reasoning fail?
instructions_for_next: (1) CRITICAL: Next cycle should lock down Haiku prefetcher output format (handles vs. content) and retrieval responsibility (prefetcher vs. controller). This is blocking concrete implementation; (2) Move from conceptual pipeline architecture to concrete Haiku prefetcher design—what exactly is the prompt, what are the inputs, what are the constraints on output size/structure; (3) Define prefetcher evaluation metrics: how do we measure triage quality? Precision, recall, reasoning outcome, cost efficiency, some combination?; (4) Consider whether prefetcher needs task context (full goal/query) or can work from shallow pattern matching. This affects design complexity.; (5) Design fallback or error handling if prefetcher filters out critical context—does reasoning retry with full context, or is it a hard failure?; (6) Once prefetcher design is concrete, can begin building integration tests: does Haiku triage correlate with expensive model's actual attention patterns or reasoning steps?; (7) Still pending: concrete operationalization of H0 success criteria. Stop-behavior from Pichay remains candidate but needs formal test protocol.; (8) If prescriptive prefetcher path reveals insights (e.g., certain types of context are always filtered, or prefetcher decisions diverge from expensive model's needs), flag implications for H0 bounded-processor framing; (9) Track whether prefetcher design suggests limitations in how much 'boundedness' can be achieved through external staging vs. internal model architecture; (10) Monitor for any signs that prefetcher + expensive model approximates or diverges from single expensive model's reasoning, as this tests whether pipeline is efficiency-modeling (descriptive) or constraint-imposing (prescriptive).
